nativeRange.habitat: The Pearly Rasbora inhabits shallow, slow-flowing lowland rainforest streams below 1000 m elevation, where heavy tree canopy keeps conditions dim and cool. The water is clear to lightly tannin-stained (a natural brown tint from decaying leaves and wood), with very low conductivity, a silty or sandy bottom, a deep accumulation of leaf litter, and dense marginal vegetation along the banks — classic blackwater stream conditions.

decorPreferences: dense marginal planting (Cryptocoryne, Java fern, mosses)
leaf litter bed (Indian almond / catappa leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)
open mid-water swimming space for schooling

diet.notes: Micropredatory in the wild, consuming small invertebrates, terrestrial insects (Diptera, Coleoptera), and organic detritus. In the aquarium, provide regular small meals of live or frozen micro-foods — Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, bloodworm, and micro-worms — supplemented with high-quality small-particle dried flakes and granules. Varied diet supports optimal coloration. Mouth is small; avoid large-particle foods.

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (with caution — fry may be predated)
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish that cause stress
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete for food

summary: The Pearly Rasbora (Rasboroides vaterifloris) is a small, brilliantly colored schooling fish endemic to Sri Lanka, reaching around 4 cm as an adult. Despite the common name, it belongs to the distinct genus Rasboroides rather than true Rasbora. It is IUCN-listed as Endangered and thrives only in dim, soft, tannin-stained blackwater conditions.
careNotes:
The Pearly Rasbora is a delicate, specialist fish suited to experienced aquarists who can replicate its soft, acidic blackwater habitat. Despite the common name, Rasboroides vaterifloris belongs to the distinct genus Rasboroides within family Danionidae and is not a true Rasbora — a distinction worth bearing in mind when sourcing livestock, since the fish is sometimes mislabeled or grouped incorrectly at retail. The species is endemic to the wet-zone river systems of southwestern Sri Lanka, principally the Kalu basin, where it inhabits cool, heavily shaded lowland rainforest streams with very low conductivity, tannin-stained water, and deep leaf litter.

Water chemistry is critical: maintain pH 5.5–7.0 with soft water (gH 1–8 dGH) and temperature 23–27°C. Hard or alkaline water tends to cause rapid decline. Use reverse-osmosis or very soft tap water; supplement with dried Indian almond (catappa) leaves or commercially prepared blackwater extract to lower pH and add tannins naturally. Avoid bright lighting — a heavily planted, shaded setup with driftwood and leaf litter best replicates the natural habitat and reduces chronic stress.

Keep in groups of at least 8 (ideally 10+): isolated individuals or small groups tend to remain stressed and rarely display full coloration. The species is retiring and can be outcompeted easily; house only with equally small, non-boisterous companions such as Boraras species, Trigonostigma species, dwarf loaches, and Otocinclus. Do not introduce to a biologically immature (uncycled) aquarium, as this species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes. Gentle filtration with a flow rate of 4–5× tank volume is appropriate; avoid strong currents.

Specimens often arrive in poor condition after shipping stress. Quarantine is strongly recommended. Wild-caught fish are reportedly becoming scarcer, and highly colored individuals are increasingly uncommon; captive-bred stock should be preferred wherever available. The IUCN classifies this species as Endangered (EN) due to habitat loss from deforestation and selective collection pressure on the most vivid wild individuals.
breedingNotes: The Pearly Rasbora is an egg scatterer that provides no parental care after spawning. To encourage spawning, condition a group of 5–6 pairs on live foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, and micro-worms for one to two weeks, then transfer them to a dedicated breeding tank set to soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, temperature around 25°C) with fine-leaved plants or java moss as spawning substrate. Pairs typically spawn over 4–5 days; adults should be removed promptly once spawning ends, as they will consume eggs and fry. Eggs hatch within 24–48 hours, and fry become free-swimming after a further 3–5 days. Begin feeding fry on infusoria or Paramecium cultures, then progress to Artemia nauplii and micro-worms as they grow. Growth is slow and sexual maturity takes several months. Fry are sensitive to water quality, so frequent small water changes using pre-conditioned soft water are important throughout the rearing period.

primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (23–27°C), pH (5.5–7.0), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ 1–8 dGH), minimum tank dimensions (60×30 cm, ~54 L), group size (6–10 minimum), diet (micropredator; live/frozen plus dried), breeding methodology (egg scatterer, 5–6 pairs, 24–48 h hatch, fry feeding progression), native range (Kalu, Bentota, Gin, Nilwala basins, SW Sri Lanka), habitat (shaded lowland forest streams, leaf litter, low conductivity), compatible tankmates, common names, and care warnings regarding water sensitivity and import stress.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.0 cm TL), temperature (25–29°C), pH (~6.0), hardness (5–8 dH), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, subfamily Rasborinae), native range (Kalu basin, Sri Lanka), habitat (shallow cool shaded forest streams, silty substrate, leaf debris), diet (terrestrial insects, detritus), IUCN status (Endangered EN, assessed August 2019, criteria B1ab(iii)+2ab(iii)).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rasboroides vaterifloris
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Endangered status (2020 assessment), endemic range restricted to Kalu basin per 2013 phylogeographic revision, synonymy of R. nigromarginatus with R. vaterifloris established 2018, max size 4 cm TL, common names (pearly rasbora, fire rasbora, vateria flower rasbora), present in aquarium trade.

fish.breedingNotes: The Pearly Rasbora spawns by scattering eggs among fine-leaved plants or moss and does not guard them afterward. Breeding groups of 5–6 conditioned pairs are typically used in a dedicated soft, acidic setup (pH 6.0–6.5). Eggs hatch within 24–48 hours and fry become free-swimming after 3–5 days. Rearing is slow and water-quality sensitive; fry should be started on Paramecium or infusoria and gradually moved onto Artemia nauplii. Adults must be removed as soon as the spawning period ends to prevent egg predation.
